description: This optical micrograph presents a metallographic cross-section of the oxide scale formed on Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after 24 hours of oxidation at 900°C in dry air. The image reveals a relatively flat and uniform oxide-metal interface without the presence of nodules or internal oxidation zones, which contrasts sharply with the nodular morphology observed at lower temperatures (600–800°C). The scale bar indicates 200 μm. The underlying alloy substrate displays a two-phase microstructure consisting of a ferrite matrix with dispersed carbide particles (K-phase). The absence of nodules and internal oxidation at 900°C, as visually confirmed in this micrograph, indicates that the aluminum flux in the alloy is sufficient to establish a stable, continuous protective Al₂O₃ scale at this temperature. This microstructural evidence supports the kinetic findings that the oxidation rate decreases with increasing temperature above 700°C due to the formation of a healing alumina layer that suppresses iron cation outward diffusion and eliminates internal oxidation.
